preFix.title: Fitting skirting board with coped internal corners
preFix.subtitle: Cut and fix timber skirting using coped joints at internal corners and mitres at external corners
preFix.excerpt: How to measure, cut, and fix timber skirting board to a room, covering coped joints at internal corners cut with a coping saw, external mitres on a mitre box, fixing by adhesive and nailing, and caulking before decoration.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Carpentry for Boys (1914).

content.text: A room has two types of corner: internal (walls meeting inward) and external (walls turning outward, as at a bay or pillar). Internal corners are best joined with a
coped joint
rather than a mitre. External corners take a 45-degree mitre, sometimes finished with a short
return end
piece to close the exposed end grain.
What you will need: materials

Skirting board in a matching profile (ogee, torus, chamfered, or square edge), 18 mm or 25 mm PAR softwood. Buy 10% more than the measured run to allow for mitre waste and coping cuts. Oval wire nails, 50 mm, for fixing to stud walls; construction adhesive for masonry walls. Decorator's caulk for the joint between the skirting top edge and the wall plaster.
Step 1, plan the fixing sequence

Work around the room in one direction. The first piece butts straight into both corners of its wall with no profile cut needed. Each subsequent piece is coped at its internal corner end to fit over the face of the piece already fixed. External corners are mitred when you reach them. This sequence means each piece you fit only has one coped end to cut, never two at the same time.
Step 2, cut the coped joint

Cut the coping piece 20 mm longer than the wall run. At the internal-corner end, cut a 45-degree internal mitre with the mitre saw. The mitre reveals the full profile of the board as a scribed line on its face. Follow this line with the coping saw, holding the saw at a slight back-angle (about 5 degrees away from the face) to create a slight undercut behind the visible edge. Test the cope dry against the installed piece: it should close without a gap along the full height of the profile.
Step 3, cut external mitres and return ends

At an external corner, cut the two meeting pieces at 45 degrees in opposite directions so the mitre closes on the corner of the wall. Check the actual angle of the corner with a sliding bevel before cutting: many apparently square corners are 91 or 92 degrees. A short return end conceals the raw end grain at a stopped run: cut a 45-degree mitre on the main piece, then cut a short off-cut with a matching mitre and glue it to the mitre face. The outer end of the return is cut square, flush with the wall plaster.
Step 4, fix the skirting

On masonry walls: apply a zigzag bead of construction adhesive to the back face, press into position, and hold with masking tape while the adhesive grips. Drive 50 mm screws or masonry nails through the skirting at stud or nogging positions. On stud walls: drive 50 mm oval nails or screws at 600 mm centres into the studs. Punch all nail heads below the surface with a nail punch before filling.
Step 5, fill and caulk

Fill all nail holes with one-coat filler and sand smooth when dry. Run a fine bead of decorator's caulk along the top edge of the skirting where it meets the wall plaster and tool it flush with a damp finger. This is the joint most prone to cracking with seasonal movement; flexible caulk handles the movement where rigid filler would split.
Variations

A taller skirting (150 mm or 225 mm) requires a mid-height fixing as well as the top and bottom. MDF skirting takes paint very smoothly but cuts differently from softwood: the coping saw blade loads with dust quickly and must be cleared. For bare timber floors, set the skirting position before the floor is laid and leave a 5 mm expansion gap between the board bottom and the floor.
Care

Repaint the skirting at each full room redecoration. Run fresh caulk along the top edge if the bead has cracked: seasonal timber movement opens this joint every year in a centrally heated house. Use flexible decorator's caulk here, not rigid filler.

attrs.fix: Pare the high spots back with the block plane or a sharp chisel. Mark the high spots with a pencil against the installed piece and remove them until the joint closes cleanly.
attrs.cause: The coping-saw cut drifted back from the mitre line, leaving a flat section where the profile should be curved.
attrs.symptom: Coped joint has a visible gap at the top or bottom of the profile

attrs.fix: Wedge the skirting against the wall while the adhesive sets, using a timber offcut and masking tape. Add a screw into a wall plug in masonry if the spring is persistent.
attrs.cause: The wall is not plumb or there is a hollow behind the plaster; the adhesive has not bonded to a dusty or painted surface.
attrs.symptom: Skirting springs away from the wall at mid-span after fixing

attrs.fix: Inject a small amount of PVA into the gap, press the mitre closed, and drive a pin through the top of the joint to hold it while the glue sets. Fill the pin hole and repaint.
attrs.cause: Timber shrinkage has opened the mitre; or the corner angle is more than 90 degrees and the mitre was cut at 45 degrees each side.
attrs.symptom: External mitre opens a gap after the room is painted

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
